item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't upvote a skip that I didn't "get" even after reading it twice. I don't like to depend on comment section explanations. However, if it's as Scorpion451 said 4 years ago then it's really great! I just wish it was a little clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The exception to this is species of ragworms, which will be met with extreme hostility"

Immediately +1 when I read this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And if we could get some sort of hint of this in the story, this skip would be better. Till then, no vote stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, that actually makes much more sense and makes this a lot cooler.

Still not gonna +1 because reading the article twice didn't get that across… but I still like the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My working theory
– hide block

SCP-2907 is an entity that exists across two dimensions/planes of existence/perceptions of reality/you get the idea.

On the Foundation's side, this thing appears to be a dead but intact specimen of an extinct priapulid worm species that mind controls worms. The worms apparently don't like this, as they will not return to the area of mind control after being removed from it. It, by turn, does not like ragworms, which may be immune to its effects.

On the other side, however, reality is very different:

  • SCP-2907 seems to be some sort of eldritch abomination contained by a Foundation counterpart, for which SCP-2907 and its earthworm minions represent a keter threat.
  • Sapient entities on this side may possibly be perceived as worms on the Foundation's side. They may or may not actually be sentient worms.
  • Regardless, to them 2907's zone of influence is basically a mind controlling hellgate spawning mind-slaved minions.
  • The extra worms that appear if it doesn't get it's minimum quota may come from their side as well- say, it temporarily expands its sphere of influence or something.
  • Periodically, the other side's Foundation counterpart must execute some drastic "hail mary" containment measure to hold the thing back, reflected by the controlled worms turning against SCP-2907 and destroying it before dispersing.
  • This is very difficult to do, however, and they have apparently been fighting a loosing war against SCP-2907 for some time now.

Since containment, however, "our" foundation had been isolating SCP-2907, and preventing it from freely enslaving endless numbers of earthworms- limiting it to its minimum production of five worms per day (which may possibly be recruited from the other side if none are available on this side). Essentially, on their side it would seem like a hellgate pouring out demons suddenly slowed to a trickle. This has apparently allowed the other side to turn the tide against SCP-2907, and they seriously wounded it and damaged its ability to call new minions on 09/06/86.

The second message was them saying "thank you" and "keep it up, it's helping!" to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm afraid I just don't understand this one. I mean, I understand what it does, but not the implications of the voice or events. Otherwise, it is interesting. +0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The frequency of Zytling events has decreased since containment started with the longest time being 926 days but the overall average trending longer. I am not sure how you are getting three and a half years from that. The minimum time is forty days (if a Zytling event occurs immediately after another one) and the maximum is (so far) 926 days, we aren't told the average, but three and a half years for 32 events is clearly impossible. You seem to be assuming that a Zytling event occurs every forty days, but it clearly doesn't. Also if containment started in '78 there's eight years of containment by the second addenda.

This could be written anytime between the 1986 date and 2058.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Articles don't have to be set in the present day, there are certainly some set in earlier years and others hundreds of years in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2907-1 does not undergo decomposition, and its state has remained unchanged since containment began in 1978

After this, no instances will display any evidence of being under SCP-2907's influence. SCP-2907-1 will reconstitute itself over a period of approximately forty days, after which its compulsive effect returns. During SCP-2907's containment, thirty two Ziying Events have occured.

Given those together, we can tell they've had it for a little over three and a half years and that the current year is somewhere in the 80s. Not sure if this is still being monitored and it's really pedantic to even bring this up, but it's kinda sloppy for the Foundation to leave this sitting around unupdated for decades. You might consider upping the number of events to something less wildly off for the timeframe (unless it was supposed to stop in the 80s).

Containment procedures are also kind funky. It's kind of just being fed worms? Why not lock it in a metal box with five/two worms and forget about it? I'm not getting why it's being given free reign. The voice also seems really random.

Ended up without a whit of idea what's going on, despite the random narration. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmyeah, terribly sorry. I don't get this one. Well written in a good clinical tone, but… Aungiddit.

+-0'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given you apparently upvoted, you might wanna reverse that, then…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't quite like it, and I don' think I really get it. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for breaking the seven-article streak of total s*** I just read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An SCP that does Weird Stuff to organisms over than humans! Humans don't even seem to matter to it!

Automatic +1, even if the article wasn't unusual and interesting, which it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I can say this is the first article I've read in which a prehistoric worm corpse telepathically enslaves other worms. +1

although they will display extreme aversion to returning.

Mentally picturing a little worm furiously trying to wiggle away from a researcher trying to put it back in the area of effect.

EDIT: spelling error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reworded those bits slightly to sound better. Not so much the interest factor, though, sorry. Thanks for the review!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not amazing, but totally solid and it even brought a smile to my face. :) Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Down voted for some tone/grammar issues:

SCP-2907-1 is the carcass of an unidentified species of priapulid worm, similar in appearance to Ottoia prolifica, which is a species known to have been extinct since the Middle Cambrian period, approximately 505 million years ago.

Really long sentence that would probably sound better broken up a bit. Clinical tone is off for me where it says "which is a species known to have been…"

Upon contact, they will become instances of SCP-2907-2, either -2A or -2B.

Incomplete sentence that breaks clinical tone.

Just not that interesting over all. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well written, and maintains a nice, solid, clinical tone. Also, it's just weird. Not scary-weird, or unearthly-weird, just, weird-weird. I dig weird-weird.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit that even after reading your explanation on the forum I still don't entirely understand the backstory… No matter though, it's weird and good enough that I like it anyway :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Hold on, how can the worms embark on a Great Leap Forward… if they have no legs?!

In all seriousness, though, this is a pleasantly weird idea that you get across in a neat, concise package.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was inspired by my attempt to explain the difference between sentience and sapience to someone.

+ Laundry list of reviewers
- If you're not here, sorry for forgetting you...

Many thanks to Twisted Gears, Dr_Leonard, LoganArmstrong, Smapti, Prior, Petrograd, Cyclopian, Dr_Sanford and probably like millions more!


Special thanks to Cimmerian, Decibelle and thedeadlymoose for their ideas and help, and Pincier, for reviewing so much and harrassing me into posting this.
